item.title: Designing a Distributed Rate Limiter
item.author: Ashish Pratap Singh
item.content:encoded: A is a mechanism used to control the number of requests or operations a user, client, or system can perform within a specific time window.Its primary purpose is to ensure of resources, , and from being overwhelmed by sudden spikes in traffic. If a system allows a maximum of , any request beyond that limit within the same minute would either be or , often with an HTTP response.In this article, we will dive into the system design of a , and explore the the 5 most commonly used with examples, pros and cons.Before diving into the architecture, lets outline the functional and non-functional requirements:1.1 Functional RequirementsEnforce a fixed number of requests per user or API key within a defined time window (e.g., 100 requests per minute). Excess requests should be rejected with an HTTP .Limits must be enforced consistently across all nodes in a distributed environment. Users shouldn't bypass limits by switching servers.Apply limits across multiple time granularities simultaneously (e.g., per second, per minute, per hour) to prevent abuse over short and long bursts.1.2 Non-Functional RequirementsTo be usable at scale, our distributed rate-limiter must meet several critical non-functional goals:The system should scale horizontally to handle massive request volumes and growing user counts.Rate limit checks should be fast ideally adding no more than a few milliseconds per request.The rate-limiter should continue working even under heavy load or node failures. There should be no single point of failure.All nodes should have a of each user’s request counts. This prevents a client from bypassing limits by routing requests through different servers.The system should support a large number of operations per second and serve many concurrent clients without significant performance degradation.The acts as a between the client and the backend servers. Its job is to inspect incoming requests and enforce predefined usage limits (e.g., 100 requests per minute per user or IP).To apply these limits effectively, the rate limiter must for each client. These counts are often maintained across , such as per second, per minute, or per hour.Using a traditional relational database for this purpose is generally unsuitable due to:: Relational databases involve disk I/O, which introduces delays on every read/write.: Handling thousands of concurrent updates (e.g., one per incoming request) can lead to locks and race conditions.: RDBMSs are not optimized for high-frequency, real-time counter updates. An like is a far better fit for rate limiting use cases because it offers: for both reads and writes like , , and , ensuring safe concurrent updates without race conditionsTTL (Time-to-Live) support, allowing counters to reset automatically at the end of each time window (e.g., after 60 seconds for a per-minute limit)Here’s how the rate limiter fits into the flow of an incoming request: to an endpoint of the application.The performs several checks:Identifies the client (via IP, token, or API key)Looks up the current request count in Redis (or in-memory cache)Applies any (e.g., free vs premium users)If the count exceeds the allowed threshold, the request is with HTTP 429 Too Many Requests.If the count is , the counter is incremented and the request proceeds to the backend service.Periodically, via TTL or are reset based on window granularity.Many modern applications delegate rate limiting to such as or , which can efficiently enforce limits before traffic reaches backend services. However, for this discussion, we will focus on designing a that is integrated into or called by directly.3.1 Single-Node Rate LimitingFor small-scale applications with low traffic and a single application server, rate limiting can be implemented entirely , without relying on external systems like Redis. This approach is lightweight, fast, and easy to set up.You maintain a simple in the application process where: represent client identifiers (e.g., user ID, API key, or IP address) represent request counts within the current time windowFor each incoming request:Checks if the user exists in the mapIf not, create a new entry with a count of 1If the user exists, increments their counterCompare the count against the defined rate limitIf the count is within the limit, allow the request; otherwise, reject itYou can also add a time-based mechanism (e.g., timestamps or TTL logic) to reset counters after each time window.Despite its simplicity, this approach comes with that make it unsuitable for production environments at scale:Single Point of Failure (SPOF): If the server crashes, all in-memory counters are lost. After a restart, the system "forgets" users' recent request history potentially allowing them to exceed their limits until the counters rebuild.No Horizontal Scalability: The rate limiter lives on a so it doesn’t scale with traffic.Without proper eviction or TTL logic, memory usage can grow unbounded over time, especially if you're tracking many users or long-duration windows.Now, lets explore two common strategies to implement rate limiting in a distributed environment.3.2 Distributed Rate Limiting

item.title: Cofun with Cofree Comonads • David Laing • YOW! 2015
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This presentation was recorded at YOW! 2015. #GOTOcon #YOW https://yowcon.com
David Laing - Senior Software Engineer at Skedulo
RESOURCES https://www.linkedin.com/in/david-laing http://comonad.com/reader/2008/the-cofree-comonad-and-the-expression-problem http://blog.sigfpe.com/2014/05/cofree-meets-free.html
RECOMMENDED BOOKS Edsger W. Dijkstra • A Discipline of Programming • https://amzn.to/3JlwHV6 Rebecca Skinner • Effective Haskell • https://amzn.to/3SxTpwY Uberto Barbini • From Objects to Functions • https://amzn.to/4cMDOmH Richard Feldman • Elm in Action • https://amzn.to/387kujI Tim McNamara • Rust in Action • https://amzn.to/3ux2R9u

item.title: The One Weird Trick for Analyzing Big Data…Eyeball it Early & Often! • John Lamping • YOW! 2016
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This presentation was recorded at YOW! 2016. #GOTOcon #YOW https://yowcon.com
John Lamping - Technical Advisor at Falkonry
RESOURCES https://github.com/johnlamping https://www.linkedin.com/in/john-lamping-156bb9b
ABSTRACT As programmers, we tend to treat data as generic stuff to feed into the #algorithms and #architectures we love. We don’t really pay attention to the data itself, especially when we have terabytes or petabytes of it.
Huge mistake. And we are trained to make it! It is why it takes a year for a new programmer to be productive at working on the #GoogleRanking algorithm. It held back progress on genome #SequencingAlgorithms. It has cost me more time than I’d care to imagine.
The good news is that you don’t have to look at all of your petabytes of data. Just eyeball a ten record sample when you start, and repeat as you work the data. Even then, eyeballing can be hard work, and done wrong can be worse than doing nothing. But done right, it can be fun, and the data will almost always surprise you. Better yet, you can use your favorite algorithms and architectures to build tools to make eyeballing your data easier and much more effective.
RECOMMENDED BOOKS Kevlin Henney • 97 Things Every Programmer Should Know • https://amzn.to/2Yahf9U Henney & Monson-Haefel • 97 Things Every Software Architect Should Know • https://amzn.to/3pZuHsQ Matthew Skelton & Manuel Pais • Team Topologies • http://amzn.to/3sVLyLQ Michael Jackson • Software Requirements and Specifications • https://amzn.to/3ql2T14 Geoffrey West • Scale • http://amzn.to/3eKMbpc Donald G. Reinertsen • The Principles of Product Development Flow • https://amzn.to/3hJ2Ye
https://bsky.app/profile/gotocon.com https://twitter.com/GOTOcon https://www.linkedin.com/company/goto- https://www.instagram.com/goto_con https://www.facebook.com/GOTOConferences #BigData #JohnLamping #YOWcon

item.title: Tidy First? A Daily Exercise in Empirical Design • Kent Beck • GOTO 2024
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This presentation was recorded at GOTO Chicago 2024. #GOTOcon #GOTOchgo https://gotochgo.com
Kent Beck - Software Engineer & Creator of Extreme Programming @KentLBeck
RESOURCES https://bsky.app/profile/kentbeck.bsky.social https://www.kentbeck.com https://github.com/KentBeck https://twitter.com/KentBeck https://www.linkedin.com/in/kentbeck
ABSTRACT Software design is an exercise in human relationships. What are the economic & technical factors shaping this most creative, most leveraged of programming acts?
We'll discuss coupling & cohesion, the unlikely cost of large changes, & the timing of investment in software design. [...]
TIMECODES 00:00 Intro 01:15 The idea behind Tidy First? 08:24 Why software design is a hard problem 09:44 People 15:13 Money 24:35 Software 36:12 Empirical software design 37:07 Consequences 46:00 Q&A 48:26 AI rant 51:18 Outro
Read the full abstract here: https://gotochgo.com/2024/sessions/3432
RECOMMENDED BOOKS Kent Beck • Tidy First? • https://amzn.to/4gscjjK Kent Beck & Cynthia Andres • Extreme Programming Explained • https://amzn.to/3sBASDG Kent Beck • Test Driven Development • https://amzn.to/3U4AXLs Kent Beck, Fowler, John, William, Don & Gamma • Refactoring • https://amzn.to/3SFBYbN Kent Beck • Implementation Patterns • https://amzn.to/3sBlCGL Luca Dellanna • Ergodicity • https://amzn.to/43UeRlx Edward Yourdon & Larry Constantine • Structured Design • https://amzn.to/40aWnwI

item.title: Kotlin for Developers • Marcin Moskala & Nicola Corti
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This interview was recorded for the GOTO Book Club. http://gotopia.tech/bookclub
Read the full transcription of the interview here (https://gotopia.tech/episodes/353) Marcin Moskala - Fullstack Kotlin Developer, Founder of Kt. Academy & Author of "Effective Kotlin", et al. Nicola Corti - Staff Android Engineer at Meta, React Native Core Team Member & Open Source Lover
RESOURCES Marcin https://x.com/marcinmoskala https://kt.academy https://github.com//marcinmoskala https://www.instagram.com/cortinico
Nicola https://x.com/cortinico https://ncorti.com https://github.com/cortinico https://www.linkedin.com/in/cortinico
Links https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/compose/kotlin https://detekt.dev https://www.abreslav.com https://kt.academy/article/ek-composition https://kt.academy/article/books-kotlin-for-developers-ready
DESCRIPTION In this episode of the "GOTO Book Club", Nicola Corti talks to Marcin Moskala, author of "Effective Kotlin", to unravel the inspiration, evolution, and community impact of his work.
Moskala reflects on his decade-long journey with Kotlin, revealing how his book bridges the gap between Java-centric practices and Kotlin’s idiomatic potential. The discussion spans essential topics like factory functions, composition over inheritance, and the iterative process behind curating best practices tailored to Kotlin’s strengths.
Marcin also highlights the "Kotlin for Developers" series and his Give IT Forward initiative, showcasing his commitment to empowering developers and fostering accessible programming education worldwide.
RECOMMENDED BOOKS Marcin Moskala • Effective Kotlin (https://amzn.to/40OIDa3) Marcin Moskala • Kotlin Essentials (https://amzn.to/4heEW46) Marcin Moskala • Functional Kotlin (https://amzn.to/40TVJ6M) Marcin Moskala • Kotlin Coroutines (https://amzn.to/3EcdTYT) Marcin Moskala • Advanced Kotlin (https://amzn.to/4aDzJR2) Marcin Moskala & Igor Wojda • Android Development with Kotlin (https://amzn.to/40SfN9J) Venkat Subramaniam • Programming Kotlin (https://amzn.to/2MIC8D1)
Bluesky (https://bsky.app/profile/gotocon.com) Twitter (https://twitter.com/GOTOcon) Instagram (https://www.instagram.com/goto_con) LinkedIn (https://www.linkedin.com/company/goto-) Facebook (https://www.facebook.com/GOTOConferences)
CHANNEL MEMBERSHIP BONUS Join this channel to get early access to videos & other perks: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCs_tLP3AiwYKwdUHpltJPuA/join
Looking for a unique learning experience? Attend the next GOTO conference near you! Get your ticket: gotopia.tech (https://gotopia.tech)
SUBSCRIBE TO OUR YOUTUBE CHANNEL (https://www.youtube.com/user/GotoConferences/?sub_confirmation=1) - new videos posted daily!

item.title: Simplicity • Pragmatic Dave Thomas & Sarah Taraporewalla • GOTO 2025
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This interview was recorded for the GOTO Book Club. #GOTOcon #GOTObookclub http://gotopia.tech/bookclub
Read the full transcription of the interview here: https://gotopia.tech/episodes/383
Pragmatic Dave Thomas - Pragmatic Programmer Turned Publisher @pragdave Sarah Taraporewalla - CTO APAC at Thoughtworks
RESOURCES Prag Dave https://pragdave.me https://twitter.com/pragdave https://github.com/pragdave https://linkedin.com/in/dave-thomas-53aa1057
Sarah https://sarahtaraporewalla.com https://twitter.com/sarahtarap https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahtaraporewalla https://github.com/staraporf
Links https://pragprog.com https://agilemanifesto.org https://youtu.be/taCNjiiusRk https://youtu.be/ex1H_JGiK2s https://youtu.be/a-BOSpxYJ9M https://youtu.be/xsMUuOwv7IA https://youtu.be/qjJ6LvOU7q0 https://youtu.be/48g7CRlMUD8 https://youtu.be/OzCE4m9ERvU https://youtu.be/i6chgK7yjRs https://youtu.be/sLjSGrkbIFw https://youtu.be/_9vi70yFBDg https://youtu.be/yuEbZYKgZas https://youtu.be/oejXFgvAwTA https://youtu.be/10Foa_lulK4 https://youtu.be/Ee1VcWQJfzA https://youtu.be/0AzkH8SYyOc https://youtu.be/WPCrGYjrJ1Y https://youtu.be/zDugyWSguz4 https://youtu.be/vvFdTp54gII https://youtu.be/ApAkqxHZq6o https://youtu.be/vwlV39L6iR4
DESCRIPTION Sarah Taraporewalla (CTO APAC at Thoughtworks) sits down with programming legend Dave Thomas—co-founder of The Pragmatic Programmer and co-creator of principles like DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)—to discuss his latest book "Simplicity."
Dave reveals why he believes "Agile is Dead" and shares his disillusionment with how agile practices have become rigid, corporate processes rather than the flexible, value-driven approach originally envisioned in the Agile Manifesto he helped create. The conversation centers around his new Orient-Step-Learn framework, designed to help individual developers master true simplicity through deliberate practice and feedback loops, emphasizing that real simplicity requires mastery and cannot be achieved overnight.
Dave advocates for developers to take personal agency, reduce unnecessary dependencies, and focus on what they can control rather than waiting for organizational change, arguing that simplicity is ultimately about cutting away complexity to reveal elegant, minimal solutions.
TIMECODES 00:00 Intro 03:34 Why is 2025 the year for simplicity? 09:05 Understanding true simplicity 11:52 The «Orient-Step-Learn» Framework 24:47 Organizational impact & limitations 27:54 Practical steps to get started 41:07 Outro
RECOMMENDED BOOKS Dave Thomas • simplicity • https://amzn.to/43FghBJ Dave Thomas & Andy Hunt • The Pragmatic Programmer • https://amzn.to/43QuMBj Dave Snowden & Friends • Cynefin • https://amzn.to/3FSnF3p Subramaniam & Hunt • Practices of an Agile Developer • https://amzn.to/2XjbWor Uncle Bob • Clean Agile • https://amzn.to/3tpAqb5 Derby, Larsen & Schwaber • Agile Retrospectives • https://amzn.to/3hB4eNk
https://bsky.app/profile/gotocon.com https://twitter.com/GOTOcon https://www.linkedin.com/company/goto- https://www.instagram.com/goto_con https://www.facebook.com/GOTOConferences #Simplicity #SustainableDevelopment #EffectiveDevelopment #PragmaticProgrammer #Mastery #ProgrammingMastery #SWComplexity #SWSimplicity #OODA #OrientStepLearn #TodayInTech #SoftwareDevelopment #SoftwareEngineering #Programming #DaveThomas #PragDave #PragmaticDave #SarahTaraporewalla #BookClub
CHANNEL MEMBERSHIP BONUS Join this channel to get early access to videos & other perks: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCs_tLP3AiwYKwdUHpltJPuA/join

item.title: Introducing the search feature
item.author: probabl
item.content:encoded:
skore v0.9.1 is out! We have a new search feature that allows you to navigate through your stored skore estimator reports.
------ Links:
skore v0.9 documentation: https://docs.skore.probabl.ai/0.9/index.html
skore GitHub repository: https://github.com/probabl-ai/skore
Website: https://probabl.ai/ LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/company/probabl Twitter: https://x.com/probabl_ai Bluesky: https://bsky.app/profile/probabl.bsky.social Discord: https://discord.probabl.ai
We also host a podcast called Sample Space, which you can find on your favourite podcast player. All the links can be found here: https://rss.com/podcasts/sample-space/
#probabl

item.title: Designing a large-scale vector DB like Exa AI Search
item.author: ankush
item.content:encoded:
In this video we will go over the blog written by Exa (an AI search company) about how they built their web-scale vector FB for backing AI search. This video will give you the areas to think about when you are working with vector DBs.
Link to the blog: https://exa.ai/blog/building-web-scale-vector-db
#ai #vectordatabase #database

item.title: Moldable Development: Programming Through Custom Tools • Tudor Girba • GOTO 2024
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This presentation was recorded at GOTO Copenhagen 2024. #GOTOcon #GOTOcph https://gotocph.com
Tudor Girba - Software Environmentalist and CEO of feenk
RESOURCES https://bsky.app/profile/tudorgirba.com https://www.linkedin.com/in/girba https://github.com/girba https://medium.com/@girba https://twitter.com/girba http://www.tudorgirba.com
Links https://youtu.be/zduSFxRajkE https://gtoolkit.com
ABSTRACT At first, programming was about code.
At some point, tests became code. This introduced a new feedback loop and programming changed.
Then, infrastructure became code. This too introduced a new feedback loop and programming changed again.
Now, tools are becoming code. This is also introducing a new feedback loop and programming is changing yet again.
Tools as code are tools that can be adapted and extended quickly, ideally while we program. These tools can then be customized for each development problem. The practice of programming through such custom tools is called Moldable Development.
In this talk, we'll explore what Moldable Development is through live demos of Glamorous Toolkit, a moldable development environment. [...]
TIMECODES 00:00 Intro 00:52 Moldable development 07:17 Example 15:50 Demo 23:00 Tokenization 24:44 https://youtu.be/zduSFxRajkE 26:13 Tokenization continued 34:28 Model centric 42:58 Conclusion 44:40 Outro
Read the full abstract here: https://gotocph.com/2024/sessions/3337
RECOMMENDED BOOKS Susanne Kaiser • Adaptive Systems With Domain-Driven Design, Wardley Mapping, and Team Topologies • https://amzn.to/4b7ht2n Eric Evans • Domain-Driven Design • https://amzn.to/3tnGhwm Matthew Skelton & Manuel Pais • Team Topologies • http://amzn.to/3sVLyLQ Heidi Helfand • Dynamic Reteaming • https://amzn.to/3Fvu5BA Vlad Khononov • Learning Domain-Driven Design • https://amzn.to/47O7GLx Erik Schön • The Art of Strategy • https://amzn.to/3vPmmwS Thomas M. Siebel • Digital Transformation • https://amzn.to/3elUnIV
https://bsky.app/profile/gotocon.com https://twitter.com/GOTOcon https://www.linkedin.com/company/goto- https://www.instagram.com/goto_con https://www.facebook.com/GOTOConferences #TudorGirba #MoldableDevelopment #GlamorousToolkit #Programming #SoftwareEngineering #DeveloperProductivity #DeveloperHappiness #AWSCardDemo #TodayInTech #Tokenization

item.title: Tech Leadership Challenges: Communication & AI at FT • Alice Bartlett & Charles Humble • GOTO 2025
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
This interview was recorded for GOTO Unscripted. #GOTOcon #GOTOunscripted https://gotopia.tech
Read the full transcription of this interview here: https://gotopia.tech/articles/382
Alice Bartlett - Tech Director for Customer Products at Financial Times @ab549 Charles Humble - Freelance Techie, Podcaster, Editor, Author & Consultant
RESOURCES Alice https://x.com/alicebartlett https://github.com/alicebartlett https://www.linkedin.com/in/alicebartlett https://medium.com/@alice.bartlett https://alicebartlett.co.uk
Charles https://bsky.app/profile/charleshumble.bsky.social https://linkedin.com/in/charleshumble https://mastodon.social/@charleshumble https://conissaunce.com
Links https://blog.container-solutions.com/wtf-happens-to-psychological-safety https://youtu.be/Qdits--aoT4 https://youtu.be/tMzDMrBGJlI https://youtu.be/P_6eDL1aqtA https://youtu.be/gYhJU0-j-8g https://youtu.be/w9YhmMPLQ4U https://youtu.be/hIwVqt6qtc4
DESCRIPTION Alice Bartlett, Tech Director at the Financial Times, discusses her journey from principal engineer to leading a 70-person team responsible for https://www.ft.com and mobile apps. She shares insights on managing editorial stakeholders, balancing technical debt with business priorities, leading difficult conversations, and navigating the challenges of AI in software development.
The conversation with Charles Humble covers her approach to architectural challenges, communication strategies, and the evolving role of technology leadership in modern media organizations.
TIMECODES 00:00 Intro 02:06 Editorial & commercial groups at Financial Times 07:38 From developer to tech director 14:24 Tech director responsibilities 22:45 Handling stress & dark side behaviors as a leader 25:12 How to give difficult feedback 27:52 Architecture change: From spaghetti Code to systemic clarity 37:31 Thinking 2-3 years ahead 39:35 The impact of AI on software development 46:17 Outro
RECOMMENDED BOOKS Simon Wardley • Wardley Maps • https://amzn.to/45U8Upr Simon Wardley • Wardley Mapping, The Knowledge • https://amzn.to/3XQEeDu Sun Tzu • The Art of War • https://amzn.to/2BqDeha Jonathan Allen & Thomas Blood • Reaching Cloud Velocity • https://amzn.to/3D20KkC Mark Craddock • Wardley Mapping Doctrine • https://amzn.to/4b3jRYb Susanne Kaiser • Adaptive Systems With Domain-Driven Design, Wardley Mapping & Team Topologies • https://amzn.to/3XTmNCc David Anderson, Marck McCann & Michael O'Reilly • The Value Flywheel Effect • https://amzn.to/3VcHxC7 Erik Schön • The Art Of Strategy • https://amzn.to/2UPzXPa
https://bsky.app/profile/gotocon.com https://twitter.com/GOTOcon https://www.linkedin.com/company/goto- https://www.instagram.com/goto_con https://www.facebook.com/GOTOConferences #TechnicalDirector #DigitalTransformation #Strategy #AITransformation #TechnicalLeadership #SoftwareArchitecture #Communication #Storytelling #Change #FT #FinancialTimes #TodaInTech #AliceBartlett #CharlesHumble

item.title: What are JSON Web Tokens (JWTs)?
item.author: Ashish Pratap Singh

item.title: @ChristianHeilmann about #AI #CodeStandards for Programming Teams
item.author: GOTO Conferences
item.content:encoded:
Check out the full version on our YouTube channel now! #GOTOcon #DeveloperTools #DeveloperProductivity #Productivity #SoftwareEngineering #SoftwareDevelopment #Programming #GithubCoPilot #WebDevelopment #Console #DevTools #ChristianHeilmann #JulianWood #ViralProgrammingShorts #Viral #ViralShorts #TodayInTech #GOTO

Christian Heilmann - uthor of Several JS Books & Previously Worked at Yahoo, Mozilla & Microsoft @ChristianHeilmann
